KeyVaults are predominantly deployed with public network access enabled and soft-delete configured (most with 90-day retention): because KeyVaults are currently reachable over public endpoints but already use soft-delete, plan to harden secret key storage for the finance app by moving KeyVaults to private endpoints or service endpoints and enabling purge protection to prevent accidental or malicious deletion.

User-assigned managed identities are widespread and frequently co-deployed with KeyVault and application telemetry in resource-group patterns: since managed identities are already a common access model, plan to use a user-assigned managed identity for the finance web app to access KeyVault and the database to avoid embedding credentials.

Network Security Groups and Virtual Networks are routinely present alongside web apps and public IPs in frequent itemsets: because the tenant consistently places app tiers inside VNets protected by NSGs, design the finance app to run in a dedicated VNet/subnet with NSG rules that limit inbound access to only finance-team IP ranges or specific internal subnets.

App Service (web sites) resources in the tenant consistently have public network access enabled and many App Service Plans are small Basic SKUs: given the current tendency to publish web apps publicly on lower-cost plans, choose a higher-tier hosting plan (or an internal ASE/isolated plan) for the finance internal web app and disable public access or front it with an internal-only gateway for stronger isolation.

Application Gateways in the tenant are uniformly WAF_v2 SKU where present: because WAF_v2 is the standard for HTTP protection in existing deployments, plan to place the finance app behind an internal Application Gateway with WAF_v2 and an appropriate rule set to protect against web attacks.

Storage accounts most commonly use Standard_LRS and have network rules defaulting to Allow: since tenant storage favors single-region redundancy and permissive network ACLs, evaluate storage redundancy needs for finance data and tighten storage network rules (deny by default, allow specific private endpoints) and enable additional encryption-in-transit/in-rest controls if required by finance policy.

Many platform services (Cognitive Services, App Insights ingestion, etc.) have public network access enabled across the tenant: because public access is the default pattern today, apply Azure Policy or design-time controls to require private endpoints or service endpoints for sensitive finance workloads to reduce public exposure.

Application telemetry and Log Analytics (Insights components and OperationalInsights workspaces) frequently co-occur with web/serverfarm deployments: since monitoring is already standard practice, include an Application Insights instance and a dedicated Log Analytics workspace in the finance deployment for centralized telemetry, alerting, and audit trails.

SQL Servers and databases in the tenant appear in small numbers but the tenant shows usage of Hyperscale vCore SKUs for some databases: because there is precedent for Hyperscale and vCore sizing, size the relational backend for the finance app based on expected growth (consider Hyperscale if large storage/scale is anticipated) and document backup/DR given that zone redundancy is not commonly enabled.

Private endpoints and Private DNS zones are observed together with NSGs and web certificates in MFIs: because this pattern supports private connectivity and name resolution, plan to use private endpoints plus private DNS for the finance web app and its database to ensure internal-only resolution and avoid public endpoints.

KeyVaults often have enablerbacauthorization set to False while managed identities are common: since many KeyVaults still use access policies rather than Azure RBAC, evaluate moving the finance KeyVaults to Azure RBAC-based access control to simplify permission management for managed identities used by the app.

Container Registry and container hosting patterns commonly include managed identities, NSGs, ACR and monitoring: because containerized CI/CD is an established pattern, if you containerize the finance web app, include Azure Container Registry, integrate with managed identities for push/pull, secure ACR network access, and enable monitoring in the same resource group.

Public IP and Load Balancer usage in frequent itemsets shows Standard SKUs are the norm: since Standard SKU networking is preferred for predictable behavior, use Standard public IPs and Standard load balancers only where absolutely required and prefer internal load balancers or private IPs for internal-only finance endpoints.

TLS minimum versions across platform services are predominantly set to TLS1_2: because TLS1_2 is already widely enforced, require TLS1_2+ for the finance web app, database connections, and storage endpoints to meet industry best-practices and reduce attack surface.

Application Insights and App Service pairs appear frequently in many itemsets with KeyVault and storage: because observability and secret management are commonly deployed together, include App Insights, KeyVault and a secured storage account (with restricted network access) as baseline components in the finance app resource group to align with tenant conventions.
